items.q: In Milgram's study, what percentage of participants gave the maximum 450V shock?
items.why: Students remember 'most' obeyed and assume all did. Actually 65% went to 450V; 35% defied. This nuance matters because it shows obedience is strong but not absolute.

items.q: How are phobias MAINTAINED according to Mowrer's two-process model?
items.correct: Through operant conditioning - avoidance reduces anxiety (negative reinforcement).
items.misconception: Through classical conditioning continuing over time.
items.why: Classical conditioning ACQUIRES the phobia; operant conditioning MAINTAINS it. Students conflate these. Avoiding the feared object reduces anxiety, reinforcing the avoidance.

items.q: What does a correlation of -0.8 indicate?
items.misconception: A weak relationship (because it is negative).
items.why: Students treat the negative sign as 'weak'. The sign shows DIRECTION (as one variable goes up, the other goes down); the absolute value shows STRENGTH. -0.8 is strong.

items.q: Children adopted from Romanian orphanages before 6 months mostly:
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Caught up well. “Showed permanent damage” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: An AO3 weakness of Bowlby is:
items.why: Correct answer: Underplays the role of fathers. “No evidence at all” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Before what age did Romanian orphans show the best recovery?
items.why: The real figure is 6. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
